### 1. Introduction

Conventional automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems typically consist of several independently learned components: an acoustic model to predict context-dependent sub-phoneme states (senones) from audio, a graph structure to map senones to phonemes, and a pronunciation model to map phonemes to words. The paper explores two embedding techniques: word embeddings and second–order co–occurrence vectors. Word embeddings are a type of distributional approach that uses a neural network to learn a vector representation of a word based on its context. Second–order co–occurrence vectors are a type of distributional approach that uses a second–order co–occurrence vectors.
